title: Today Is Murasoli's Birthday!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The Tamil section below is a corrected reading copy, while the canonical Tamil chapter remains untouched. The source pages were consulted for the quoted passages from Anna, Kalaignar's Golden Jubilee poem, and the Professor's greeting. Clearly visible OCR errors in those passages and in names were corrected only in this translation file.
paragraphs: 10 August 1942. The moment I see 10 August on the calendar, a flood of light surges through my heart. For that was the day Murasoli was born. This 10 August marks Murasoli's seventy-third birthday. Which Murasoli? The Murasoli that began as a handbill, grew into a weekly, and has continued its march as a daily newspaper! Murasoli, which had appeared as a handwritten monthly, began to be printed as a weekly on 14 January 1948 and as a daily on 17 September 1960. Today, 10 August, Murasoli enters its seventy-third year. As for me, I have completed ninety-one years. Yes, Murasoli was born when I was eighteen!
Kindled as a small earthen lamp in 1942 and shining today like a radiant lamp in the sky, Murasoli brings a few memories to me on this day, as its founder.

Perhaps because I founded it, Murasoli, too, has passed through many trials and much anguish, just as I have in my own life. To say more, how many court cases has it faced? Why do I say this? On the very day Murasoli was born, demonstrations on behalf of our Kazhagam are being held today in every district headquarters, under the leadership of the district secretaries, in support of prohibition. When I reflect upon this fitting correspondence, I feel immense pride.

Udanpirappē, I know very well that as you stand with your head held high in the field of this demonstration, you will read this letter of mine and unfailingly remember that this day is Murasoli's birthday.

The year 1942, when I founded Murasoli, was also the year I met Perarignar Anna. My article “Sacrifice of Youth” appeared in Arignar Anna's journal Dravida Nadu in 1942. That same year, when Perarignar Anna came to Tiruvarur, he had me brought before him, praised and blessed me, and offered me advice. Thus, whenever I think of Anna, Murasoli comes to mind; whenever I remember Murasoli, Anna fills my heart, inseparably and forever.

When I was twenty, Thanthai Periyar came to the annual celebration of the Tiruvarur Self-Respect Association. He saw Murasoli and praised it as “excellent work.” That, too, remains indelible in my memory even today.
If we recall the article written by Perarignar Anna, the teacher of the world of knowledge, for the 1962 Pongal annual, today's younger generation will fully understand Murasoli's service:
Murasoli:
“Next only to music, it brings sweetness to the ears and thoughts of Tamils.

It was held in esteem in Tamil Nadu not merely as an instrument of war, but as an instrument of praise and one that proclaimed acts of generosity. Hearing it, Tamils rejoice; adversaries are confounded; and, upon final victory, Tamils celebrate.
Murasoli:

It has never refused to respect the lives and rights of others. It has never tolerated anyone setting out to destroy its own rights; it has never failed to make those who sought to rob it of its self-respect bow at its feet.
It explained valour; it was never an instrument for displaying empty noise and clamour.

Just as the instrument called the murasu stood proclaiming the valour, victory, and advancement of the Tamils, the journal form called Murasoli, a citadel of ideas filled with the artistry of younger brother Karunanidhi, has been performing with distinction the task of making us conscious of Tamil Nadu's greatness.
The country knows well that this journal resounds with the voice of ideas, the voice of epic, the voice of poetry, and the voice of purpose.
Its annual is an ornament that comes to us every year.
The annual can impart the awakening needed by those who wish to eliminate harm and those who seek a path by which the nation may prosper.
May it live; may it grow; may younger brother Karunanidhi's service triumph and flourish!”
So richly praised by Perarignar Anna, Murasoli, acclaimed by everyone at all times as my first child, was founded on this very day, seventy-three years ago, on 10 August 1942.

Today, brethren across Tamil Nadu support Murasoli with enthusiasm. At the beginning, thousands of copies were printed each month and sent to branches of the Dravidian movement in every town. With donations collected and provided by those branch organisations, Murasoli's handbill was published. Murasoli nurtured the Kazhagam, and the Kazhagam nurtured Murasoli. Murasoli also helped develop my art of writing. Murasoli has had many writers; I, however, am its permanent writer for life. “Cheran” was my pen name. At the outset, Thennan was its secretary and T.S. Rajagopal its treasurer. Neither is with us today. They will live forever in Murasoli's memory.
In the poem I wrote for Murasoli's Golden Jubilee annual, I said:
“Having drunk the milk of life from Mother Anjugam,
crawled with delight towards Ayya's principles,
grown under Anna's boundless love,
raised an anti-Hindi battle force with youthful vigour,
and come to Murasoli with the resolve of an ideal, here I stand!
O Tamil, who, in the path of the primordial Valluvar,
gave us the threefold wisdom!
Never moving even an atom's moment away,
you embrace and protect me.
On your behalf, I dedicate this Golden Jubilee annual
of the battlefield drum to all the brethren who rise with a roar;
to Mother and Father, Periyar and Anna,
who bloom forever in my heart;
and to the learned elders and wise people who offer their blessings.”
At this moment, I also recall what our Kazhagam General Secretary, our esteemed Professor, wrote in his greeting for Murasoli's Golden Jubilee:

“Murasoli, which has stood as the shield of the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam, also serves as the plough of the farm that cultivates the Tamil crop. Bearing so many governmental obstacles, court cases, and unexpected losses, Murasoli marches proudly like a tusked elephant with spears upon its face. On its Golden Jubilee, I wish that your journey may continue for many, many more years. I wish that you may live to see the day when the honour lost by our people and our language is recovered, and a path is opened for Tamils to live with their heads held high. After offering these greetings, I turn the pages of Murasoli and see in it another likeness of Kalaignar's countenance. I greet it once more: may it live forever.”

The reason for Murasoli's growth is you, my beloved brethren who are dearer to me than life, who carry that very journal as both sword and shield while leading the Kazhagam. At this moment, I offer my thanks to every one of you. Murasoli has remained the voice of the Kazhagam. Continue to nurture it so that it may carry on its pure service for the awakening of the Tamil people, the primacy of the language, and the recovery of our rights!
Murasoli journeyed over stones and thorns!
Murasoli crossed many raging forest streams!
Murasoli overcame packs of savage beasts!
Murasoli has always resounded, and still resounds today, in Tamil Nadu's political arena!
On Murasoli's seventy-third birthday, as its founder, I offer my heartfelt wishes to all its readers, my beloved brethren!
M.K.
10 August 2015
